core_concepts: Pull Day Architecture: A comprehensive pull workout targets all major back and bicep functions: vertical pulling (lats), horizontal pulling (upper back/lats), and elbow flexion (biceps).
Feeder Sets: A warm-up protocol using progressive loading across 4–5 sets to find an optimal 'groove' and identify the correct weight for an all-out set to failure.
Omni-Grip/Omni-Directional Training: Utilizing varied grip widths and cable angles within a single workout to target the complex, multi-angled musculature of the back.
Stretch-Mediated Hypertrophy: Focusing on the lengthened/stretched position of a muscle—demonstrated in the video via bottom-half preacher curls—is a primary driver of muscle thickness.
Minimalist Powerlifting: Incorporating foundational lifts like squats, bench, and deadlifts using minimum effective volume to maintain strength while prioritizing bodybuilding-focused hypertrophy.

actionable_advice.warm_up: Perform 5 minutes of cardio, arm circles, and cable external rotations. Use 4 feeder sets, gradually increasing intensity (RPE 4–10) before the failure set.
actionable_advice.sets_and_reps: Failure sets: 2 sets to failure per major movement, followed by a 30% drop set to reach higher levels of activation.
actionable_advice.frequency: Pull workout follows a Push-Pull-Legs split; aim for consistency in tracking loads week-to-week.
actionable_advice.progression_methods: Track load and reps in a logbook. Use straps on failure sets to prevent grip strength from becoming the limiting factor.
actionable_advice.recovery_advice: Allow 2-3 minutes of rest between failure sets. Implement supersets (e.g., pullovers with lat stretches) to maximize training efficiency.

scientific_claims.claim: Training a muscle in the lengthened/stretched position (e.g., bottom-half preacher curls) is more hypertrophic than training the shortened position.
Feeder sets help establish neural groove and ensure proper mind-muscle connection before high-exertion sets.

coaching_insights: Allow for fluid movement: Avoid excessive stiffness during back exercises; rocking slightly can assist in feeling the target muscle if it doesn't compromise control.
Use straps: To prioritize back hypertrophy, do not allow grip strength to dictate the volume of your back training.
Unilateral work: For bicep work, prioritize one arm at a time to prevent asymmetries and increase mental focus on the stretch.
common_mistakes: Ego lifting: Using a grip too wide to allow for full range of motion or too narrow, forcing the biceps to take over.
Neglecting the negative: Rushing the eccentric portion of the lift instead of controlling the stretch.
Stiffness: Being too rigid and preventing the lats from stretching naturally under load.
Inconsistent grip: Failing to track and vary grips appropriately across sets.

motivational_quotes: Master the groove before you master the weight.
Don't just move the load; feel the stretch.
Technical failure is just the beginning.
Growth lives in the lengthened position.
Consistency in the setup creates consistency in the gain.
Work the muscle, not the ego.
Stiffness is the enemy of the back connection.

The best back exercise is the one you can feel.
Train with fluidity, grow with intensity.
myths_addressed: Myth: Lat pullovers are a bad exercise.
Myth: You need to stay perfectly stiff on every lift.
Myth: You should always use the same grip width.
Myth: Feeder sets cause unnecessary fatigue.
Myth: You must do all the same reps for every set.
Myth: Straps are 'cheating' on back day.
Myth: Bicep growth comes only from high reps.
Myth: The back is just one simple muscle.
Myth: Powerlifting and bodybuilding cannot coexist.
Myth: You shouldn't ever use momentum.

grit_application.goal: Optimize hypertrophy via structured warm-ups and stretch-focused training.
grit_application.routine: Integrate a 'Feeder Set' toggle in the logging feature that prompts users to log 4 ramp-up sets before the 'Working Set' log.
grit_application.instruction: Add a 'Tempo/Stretch' coach card for Preacher Curls and Pullovers, reminding users to 'Hold the stretch' for optimal fiber activation.
grit_application.tracking: Use a 'Grip Width Tracker' to allow users to log their grip variation (Wide/Medium/Neutral) to monitor which angles correlate best with their progress.
